message:
fix(fuzz): fuzz_xdr filter Encode/Decode ops by initial mode

The 30-min deep fuzz_xdr campaign found a heap-buffer-overflow:
\`Scenario { initial_op_mode: 47, buf_init: [0,0,0,3], ops: [
WrapstringEncode { s: "\\u{2}\\0" }, ... ] }\` triggered a write of
~3 wire bytes into a 2-byte caller-supplied local buffer.

Root cause is a fuzz-harness bug, not a library bug. xdr_string in
DECODE mode treats \`*sp\` as a write target — when the harness's
\`WrapstringEncode\` op runs against a stream we created in DECODE
mode, xdr_string reads a u32 length from the wire (3) and writes
that many bytes into the harness's tiny local buffer. The XDR ABI
itself is correct: \`xdr_wrapstring(xdrs, sp)\` takes a single
pointer with no length, so caller is expected to either match the
stream's mode or pre-allocate appropriately.

Fix: every \*Encode op now skips itself when the stream is in
DECODE mode, and every \*Decode op skips when in ENCODE mode. The
non-string primitives (Bool/Int/Long/etc.) are mode-agnostic and
remain unfiltered. Same pattern as the earlier \`Op::Opaque\`
declared_len cap (caller can't lie about its own buffer size).

Verified: 665,233 runs in 31s with no crashes after the fix.
